op.name: isinf
op.notes: Test for infinity element-wise. Cost: numel(input).
op.summary: Test element-wise for positive or negative infinity.
op.signature: we.isinf(x, /, out=None, *, where=True, casting='same_kind', order='K', dtype=None, subok=True[, signature])

blocks.text: Test element-wise for positive or negative infinity.

Returns a boolean array of the same shape as `x`, True where ``x ==
+/-inf``, otherwise False.

items.name: out
items.type: ndarray, None, or tuple of ndarray and None, optional

desc_blocks.text:
A location into which the result is stored. If provided, it must have
a shape that the inputs broadcast to. If not provided or None,
a freshly-allocated array is returned. A tuple (possible only as a
keyword argument) must have length equal to the number of outputs.

This condition is broadcast over the input. At locations where the
condition is True, the `out` array will be set to the ufunc result.
Elsewhere, the `out` array will retain its original value.
Note that if an uninitialized `out` array is created via the default
``out=None``, locations within it where the condition is False will
remain uninitialized.

For other keyword-only arguments, see the
:ref:`ufunc docs <ufuncs.kwargs>`.

True where ``x`` is positive or negative infinity, false otherwise.
This is a scalar if `x` is a scalar.

blocks.text:
NumPy uses the IEEE Standard for Binary Floating-Point for Arithmetic
(IEEE 754).

Errors result if the second argument is supplied when the first
argument is a scalar, or if the first and second arguments have
different shapes.

blocks.code:
>>> import numpy as np
>>> np.isinf(np.inf)
True
>>> np.isinf(np.nan)
False
>>> np.isinf(-np.inf)
True
>>> np.isinf([np.inf, -np.inf, 1.0, np.nan])
array([ True,  True, False, False])

>>> x = np.array([-np.inf, 0., np.inf])
>>> y = np.array([2, 2, 2])
>>> np.isinf(x, y)
array([1, 0, 1])
>>> y
array([1, 0, 1])
